meta.title: Lambda Calculus as a Knowledge Store: Possible Worlds for AI Memory
meta.description: What if AI could tell the difference between facts, fiction, beliefs, and hypotheticals? A working prototype that uses lambda calculus and modal logic to give AI structured memory with possible worlds.
meta.author: Peter Balogh

props.title: 1. The Problem — Why AI Memory is Broken

children.children: Here's a question that should bother you: if you train a large language model on Kurt Vonnegut's Cat's Cradle, can it still reliably tell you that water freezes at 0°C?
The honest answer is: maybe not. And the reason cuts to the heart of how LLMs store knowledge.
"See the cat?" said Newt. "See the cradle?" There was no cat. There was no cradle. — Kurt Vonnegut, Cat's Cradle

LLMs store everything in weight space — facts, fiction, beliefs, hypotheticals — all superimposed in the same neural soup. The model that learned "water freezes at 0°C" from a physics textbook also learned "ice-nine causes water to freeze at room temperature" from Vonnegut. Both facts live in the same weights, with no tag saying one is ACTUAL PHYSICS and the other is FICTION.
We call this the Cat's Cradle Problem: the inability to separate ontological categories in neural knowledge storage.

props.title: Why Current Solutions Don't Fix This

children.children: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) retrieves documents, but doesn't know if a retrieved passage describes reality or fiction. If your vector database contains both a chemistry textbook and Cat's Cradle, a query about 'unusual freezing behavior' might surface either one.
Fine-tuning just pushes more patterns into weight space — still no ontological tags.
Prompt engineering ('only use factual sources') is a hope, not a mechanism. The model has no internal representation of what 'factual' means.
What we actually need: a knowledge store where every fact has a TYPE — actual vs fictional vs believed vs hypothetical — and where the system can reason about which type applies to a given query.
That's what this tutorial is about. We're going to build a knowledge store using lambda calculus that solves this problem — and it's not vaporware. The prototype exists and runs.
Don't run away. We need exactly three ideas from lambda calculus, and then we're done with the theory.
Idea 1: Functions are values. You can pass them around, store them, return them from other functions. If you've used Python lambdas or JavaScript arrow functions, you already know this.

# A function that makes functions
make_greeter = lambda name: lambda: f"Hello, {name}!"
greet_peter = make_greeter("Peter")
greet_peter()  # → "Hello, Peter!"

Idea 2: Partial application (currying). If a function takes three arguments, you can give it one and get back a function that takes two. Give it two and get back a function that takes one. This is currying, and it turns out to be incredibly powerful for knowledge representation.

# A knowledge transfer event: who gave what to whom
ATRANS = lambda giver: lambda receiver: lambda obj: {
    "type": "ATRANS",
    "giver": giver,
    "receiver": receiver,
    "object": obj
}

# Full application: a complete fact
ATRANS("Robert")("Tom")("ball")
# → {type: ATRANS, giver: Robert, receiver: Tom, object: ball}

# Partial application: a QUERY
gave_to_tom = ATRANS(?)("Tom")(?)
# → "Who gave what to Tom?" — a pattern with holes

children: The key insight: DATA IS BEHAVIOR. In Church encoding, there's no distinction between data and the functions that operate on it. A fact like 'Robert gave the ball to Tom' is literally a function — and a query like 'who gave what to Tom?' is the same function with holes (unbound variables). This is the foundation of everything that follows

props.title: Q: Can we plausibly link this to the phenomenology of human remembering?

children.children: Yes, and the connection is surprisingly deep. When Bartlett studied memory in the 1930s, he found that remembering is never retrieval of a stored record. It is reconstruction — an active process where the mind re-enacts something, filling in gaps with defaults, expectations, and context. You never pull a fact off a shelf. You regenerate it. That is strikingly close to what Church encoding does: a fact is not a static record but a function that produces answers when you apply it to a question. Remembering, in both cases, is execution.

Consider "Robert gave the ball to Tom." In a filing-cabinet model of memory, this is a row in a table, inert until some search engine finds it. But phenomenologically, that is not how you experience remembering it. If someone asks "what did Robert give?", you do not feel yourself scanning a database. You feel the event reconstitute itself, with "the ball" emerging as the salient part because the question shaped the reconstruction. The Church encoding captures exactly this: the memory is a function waiting for a question, and the question's structure (which argument you leave as a free variable) determines what the memory produces. The query literally activates the memory into giving a specific answer, just as your question literally activates the reconstruction.

children: This also explains why memories are labile and context-sensitive. If a memory is a function rather than a record, then it can behave differently depending on what you apply to it.

Ask "who was involved?" and you get Robert and Tom. Ask "what happened to the ball?" and the same memory foregrounds trajectory and transfer. Bartlett called these reconstructive schemas. In the lambda calculus framing, they are partial applications — the same function, partially applied in different ways, yielding different facets. The memory does not contain all its possible answers as stored fields. It generates them on demand. That is why this claim is foundational: if you accept that data is behavior, then memory is not storage plus search. It is a living function that only becomes a specific fact at the moment of asking — which is, phenomenologically, exactly what remembering feels like.

children.children: Asked about "This is the foundation of ever..." — 3/29/2026, 10:50:09 AM

props.title: Q: The part that freaks me out: if we can discover a syntax for mentalese, then it absolutely stands to reason that the *same* syntax would apply to the *recovery* or *generation* of thoughts (which is another way of saying "memory")

children.children: Your intuition is exactly right, and it's one of the deepest implications of this whole framework. If mentalese has a syntax — compositional rules by which meaning-bearing primitives combine — then that syntax isn't just how thoughts get built in the first place. It's also how they get rebuilt when you retrieve them. Memory and thought generation would share the same combinatorial machinery, because they're the same operation: assembling a lambda term from available parts.

Think about what this means concretely. When you originally witness Robert kicking the ball to Tom, your mind composes something like PTRANS(ball, robert, tom) — primitives snapping together according to syntactic rules. Later, when someone asks "what happened?", your mind isn't playing back a recording. It's recomposing that lambda term from stored fragments, using the same syntactic rules that built it originally. The "memory" is a regeneration process, not a lookup. And the grammar that governs how PTRANS combines with its arguments is identical whether you're perceiving, remembering, imagining, or predicting.

This is why the passage frames memories as functions rather than records. A record is static — it just sits there. A function is something you apply, and application follows syntactic rules. If you ask "who was involved?" you're applying the memory-function to a different argument than if you ask "what happened to the ball?" — and the syntax of mentalese determines what well-formed applications look like in each case. The same grammar that lets you construct the original thought lets you reconstruct it from a different angle, or even construct counterfactuals like "what if Tom had missed the ball?" That's just binding a different value to one of the variables, following the same combinatorial rules.

The freaky part, as you put it, is that this collapses a bunch of things we tend to think of as separate cognitive faculties — perception, memory, imagination, prediction — into a single syntactic engine running in different modes. Discovering the syntax of mentalese wouldn't just tell us how thoughts are structured. It would tell us how they're stored, retrieved, recombined, and generated, because all of those operations are just different ways of assembling and applying lambda terms according to the same underlying grammar.
Asked about "If a memory is a function rath..." — 3/29/2026, 10:53:47 AM

Idea 3: Composition. You can chain functions together. MAIL = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS — mailing something is an abstract transfer (ATRANS) composed with a physical transfer (PTRANS). This gives us hierarchical knowledge for free.
That's it. Three ideas. Now let's use them to build a knowledge store.
The first big design decision: facts are events, and events are immutable. Once something happened, it happened. You don't go back and edit history — you add new events.

Think of it like a bank ledger. You never erase a transaction. If there's an error, you add a correcting entry. The current balance is computed by folding over ALL the entries. This is exactly how our knowledge store works — the 'balance' is the current state of the world, computed from the full event log.
Robert gives the ball to Tom. Then Tom gives the ball to Mary. Let's see how the event log handles this:

store = LambdaStore()

# Event 1: Robert gives ball to Tom
store.assert_fact("ATRANS", giver="Robert", receiver="Tom", object="ball")

# Event 2: Tom gives ball to Mary
store.assert_fact("ATRANS", giver="Tom", receiver="Mary", object="ball")

# Who has the ball NOW? (Fold over log — last event wins)
store.query_current("object", "ball")
# → ATRANS(giver=Tom, receiver=Mary, object=ball)

# Full history of the ball
store.history("ball")
# → [Robert→Tom, Tom→Mary]

Notice: we never modified the first event. We just added a second one. The current state is computed, not stored. This means we can always answer both 'who has the ball?' AND 'who has EVER had the ball?' — something mutable stores can't do without extra bookkeeping.
Retractions work the same way. If we learn that the first event was wrong — Robert never actually gave Tom the ball — we don't delete it. We add a retraction event:

# Oops, that didn't happen
store.retract("ATRANS", giver="Robert", receiver="Tom", object="ball")

# The original event is still in the log (history is preserved)
# But query_current now skips it
store.query_current("object", "ball")
# → ATRANS(giver=Tom, receiver=Mary, object=ball)
# (Tom still gave it to Mary — that event stands)

World state = fold over the event log. This is the same principle behind event sourcing in software engineering, append-only databases, and even git. Immutability isn't a limitation — it's a superpower. It means every past state is recoverable.

Now for the real magic. Everything so far has been a single world — the actual world. But remember the Cat's Cradle Problem: we need to store facts about DIFFERENT kinds of truth. That's where possible worlds come in.

Think of possible worlds as git branches. Your main branch (w0) is the actual world — ground truth. When Peter reads Cat's Cradle, we fork a new branch (w_cats_cradle) of type FICTIONAL. When Tom forms a belief, we fork w_tom_beliefs of type EPISTEMIC. Each branch has its own event log, its own facts — and critically, its own TYPE that tells the system how to treat those facts.
The system supports six modalities — six different TYPES of truth:
ACTUAL — the real world. Water freezes at 0°C. The sky is blue. These are ground truth.
FICTIONAL — stories, novels, movies. Ice-nine freezes water at room temperature. Retrievable for literary discussion, but can NEVER pollute beliefs about the real world.
EPISTEMIC — what someone believes. Tom thinks Paris is in Germany. This is Tom's world, not the actual world. It can differ from reality and that's fine — it's a belief.
HYPOTHETICAL — 'what if?' reasoning. Temporary branches for counterfactual exploration. What if gravity were twice as strong? Forked, explored, maybe discarded.
DEONTIC — what SHOULD be true. Obligations, rules, norms. Scientists should not create ice-nine. This is prescriptive, not descriptive.
REMEMBERED — was true but might be stale. Tom's phone number used to be 555-1234. It might have changed. Flagged for potential staleness.

props.title: The Cat's Cradle Scenario — Full Code

children.children: Here's the moment where it all comes together. Watch how the modal store handles the exact scenario that breaks LLMs:

# Actual world: water freezes at 0°C
store.assert_fact("PROPATTR", entity="water",
    property="freezing_point", value="0°C", world="w0")

# Peter reads Cat's Cradle (event in actual world)
store.assert_fact("READING", agent="Peter",
    object="Cats_Cradle", world="w0")

# Fork a fictional world
store.fork_world("w0", "w_cats_cradle",
    Modality.FICTIONAL, source="Cat's Cradle")

# In the novel: ice-nine freezes water at room temperature
store.assert_fact("PROPATTR", entity="ice_nine",
    property="freezing_point",
    value="room_temperature", world="w_cats_cradle")
Now the critical queries — the ones that LLMs get wrong:

# Query: Does water freeze at room temperature?
store.query("PROPATTR", world="w0",
    property="freezing_point", entity="water")
# → 0°C  (actual world answers correctly: NO)

# Query: Is there a BOOK about water freezing at room temp?
store.query_across_worlds("PROPATTR",
    modalities=[Modality.FICTIONAL],
    property="freezing_point",
    value="room_temperature")
# → Found in w_cats_cradle: ice_nine freezes at room_temperature
#   Source: Cat's Cradle (FICTIONAL)

See the difference? The system doesn't just KNOW both facts — it knows which world each fact belongs to and what KIND of world it is. Fictional knowledge is quarantined. It can be retrieved when you ask about fiction, but it can never contaminate the actual world.
Let's push the Cat's Cradle scenario further. This is where the modal store really shines — handling the messy interplay between fiction, belief, and reality.
Tom reads Cat's Cradle. Before reading, Tom's epistemic world (what Tom believes) inherits from the actual world:

# Tom's belief world starts as a branch of actual reality
store.fork_world("w0", "w_tom_beliefs",
    Modality.EPISTEMIC, agent="Tom")

# Tom reads the novel (actual-world event)
store.assert_fact("READING", agent="Tom",
    object="Cats_Cradle", world="w0")

# Tom KNOWS it's fiction — he doesn't believe ice-nine is real
# His epistemic world still says water freezes at 0°C
store.query("PROPATTR", world="w_tom_beliefs",
    property="freezing_point", entity="water")
# → 0°C (Tom's beliefs are NOT polluted by fiction)
But now something interesting — what if Tom is confused? What if he half-remembers something about ice-nine and starts to wonder if it's real?

# Tom forms a confused belief
store.assert_fact("PROPATTR", entity="ice_nine",
    property="exists", value="maybe",
    world="w_tom_beliefs")

# We can now detect the inconsistency!
store.diff_worlds("w0", "w_tom_beliefs")
# → Divergence: w_tom_beliefs contains
#   ice_nine.exists = maybe
#   w0 has no such fact
#   Source trace: originated from FICTIONAL world w_cats_cradle

The system can trace exactly WHERE a belief came from. If Tom's epistemic world contains something that originated in a FICTIONAL world, that's a flag — it means fiction leaked into belief. A cognitive agent could use this to self-correct.

props.title: Deontic Reasoning — What Should Be True

children.children: Beyond fiction and belief, the modal store handles obligation — what SHOULD be true:

# Fork a deontic world (what should be)
store.fork_world("w0", "w_ethics",
    Modality.DEONTIC)

# Scientists should not create ice-nine
store.assert_fact("OBLIGATION",
    agent="scientists",
    action="create", object="ice_nine",
    polarity="negative",
    world="w_ethics")

# In the fictional world, Felix Hoenikker DID create it
# We can detect the violation:
store.check_deontic_violation(
    actual_world="w_cats_cradle",
    deontic_world="w_ethics")
# → VIOLATION: Felix Hoenikker created ice_nine
#   (violates: scientists should not create ice_nine)
Even though the violation happens in a fictional world, the system can reason about it. This enables literary analysis, ethical reasoning, and normative AI behavior — all from the same framework.

World diff is exactly like git diff, but between realities. You can compare what Tom believes vs what's actually true, what should be true vs what is true, or what a novel says vs what science says. Every divergence has a traceable origin.

props.title: 6. Why Lambda Calculus is the Right Abstraction

children.children: You might be wondering: why lambda calculus? Why not just use a database? The answer is that lambda calculus gives us something no other formalism does — computation and data in the same representation.

props.title: Lambda Store vs. The Alternatives
props.prompt: For each alternative, think about what it CAN'T do that the lambda store can. Then reveal the answer.

scenarios.reveal: Triple stores represent facts as subject-predicate-object triples. They handle actual-world knowledge well. But they have NO native concept of possible worlds — (water, freezing_point, 0°C) and (ice_nine, freezing_point, room_temp) would coexist with no way to tag one as fictional. Named graphs are a partial workaround but add complexity without the modal type system. And triples can't express composition: MAIL = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS has no natural representation.

Vector DBs find similar content but have ZERO semantic structure. The embedding for 'water freezes at room temperature' (from Cat's Cradle) would be VERY close to 'water freezes at 0°C' (from physics) — they'd show up in the same query results. Cosine similarity can't distinguish fact from fiction. There's no mechanism for modal types, belief tracking, or event sourcing.

scenarios.input: SQL Database: INSERT INTO facts (subject, predicate, object, world) ...
scenarios.reveal: SQL can add a 'world' column, and that gets you surprisingly far. But SQL can't represent partial queries as data (curried functions), can't compose facts (MAIL = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS), and can't express modal operators as first-class values. The lambda store's □P = λw. ∀w'. accessible(w,w') → P(w') is a QUERY and a COMPUTATION in one — SQL can't do that.

scenarios.input: Lambda Store: ATRANS = λgiver. λreceiver. λobject. Event
scenarios.reveal: Human-readable AND machine-executable. Facts and queries are the SAME type (lambda terms with different binding states). Composition is natural: build complex events from primitives. Modal operators ARE lambda terms — they're not metadata bolted on, they're part of the representation itself. The type system (FICTIONAL, EPISTEMIC, etc.) prevents category errors at the structural level, not as an afterthought.

children.children: The deep reason: in lambda calculus, the modal operators ARE lambda terms.

Necessity (□):  □P = λw. ∀w'. accessible(w,w') → P(w')
"P is true in ALL accessible worlds"

Possibility (◇): ◇P = λw. ∃w'. accessible(w,w') ∧ P(w')
"P is true in SOME accessible world"

Belief (B_a):    B_a(P) = λw. ∀w'. w' ∈ beliefs(a,w) → P(w')
"Agent a believes P in all their belief-worlds"

These aren't just mathematical notation — they're executable code. The necessity operator is a function that takes a proposition and checks it against all accessible worlds. The belief operator checks a proposition against an agent's belief worlds. The type system ensures you can't accidentally treat fictional-world facts as actual-world facts.

Composition is natural: MAIL = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS (mailing = abstract transfer composed with physical transfer). Complex events are built from primitives using function composition — the same operation that lambda calculus was literally invented for.

Here's the thing that should excite you: LLMs have a 'dead brain' problem. Between prompts, they stop thinking. There's no persistent state, no ongoing reasoning, no continuous cognition. Each conversation starts from scratch (plus whatever context you paste in).
The lambda knowledge store offers a path out of this. Here's how:

Lambda bindings as lightweight learning: When the system learns a new fact, it creates a lambda binding — no weight updates needed, no fine-tuning, no retraining. The fact is immediately available with full modal typing.

SSM hidden state as a lambda environment: State Space Models (Mamba, etc.) maintain a hidden state between tokens. That hidden state could BE a lambda environment — a set of active bindings that persist across the conversation and update with each new fact.

Structured memory for agents: AI agents that take actions in the world need to distinguish between 'what is true', 'what the user believes', 'what I hypothesize', and 'what should be true'. The modal store gives them this for free.
Cross-session continuity: The event log persists. Fork a hypothetical world in session 1, resume reasoning about it in session 5. The lambda store is the agent's long-term structured memory.

props.title: The Path from Prototype to Production

children.children: The prototype exists. It runs. You can create worlds, assert facts, fork branches, query across modalities, and detect belief contamination. What's still on the frontier:
Neural integration — connecting the lambda store to a neural network's hidden state so that structured knowledge and learned representations work together.
Scale — the current implementation is a research prototype. Production would need efficient indexing, distributed storage, and fast modal queries.
Automatic world creation — right now worlds are forked explicitly. An intelligent system should detect when it's reading fiction and fork a FICTIONAL world automatically.
Belief revision — when evidence changes, how should beliefs propagate across worlds? This is active research in both AI and philosophy.

This is active research — the prototype exists and runs, the neural integration is the frontier. We're not describing a dream; we're describing a working system that needs to be connected to the right neural substrate. The pieces exist. The theory is sound. The engineering is the next step.

The vision: an AI system that doesn't just store information, but KNOWS what kind of information it is. That can tell you about ice-nine without confusing it with real physics. That can hold beliefs, entertain hypotheticals, and reason about obligations — all while keeping its ontological categories clean.
See the cat? See the cradle? With the right knowledge store, an AI can finally see both — and know which one is real.

props.title: Build Your Own World Graph
props.prompt: Trace through each step of world-building. At each stage, think about: what's in each world? What queries would return different results depending on which world you ask?

scenarios.input: Step 1: Create the actual world (w0) with basic facts
scenarios.reveal: w0 contains: water.freezing_point = 0°C, Paris.country = France, gravity.value = 9.8 m/s². This is ground truth. Every other world branches from here and inherits these facts unless explicitly overridden.

scenarios.input: Step 2: Peter reads Cat's Cradle → fork w_cats_cradle (FICTIONAL)
scenarios.reveal: w_cats_cradle inherits all of w0, then adds: ice_nine.freezing_point = room_temperature, ice_nine.creator = Felix_Hoenikker. Query 'freezing_point of water' in w_cats_cradle still returns 0°C (inherited from w0). Query 'freezing_point of ice_nine' only exists in this branch.

scenarios.input: Step 3: Tom believes Paris is in Germany → fork w_tom_beliefs (EPISTEMIC)
scenarios.reveal: w_tom_beliefs inherits all of w0, then overrides: Paris.country = Germany. Now: query Paris.country in w0 → France. Query Paris.country in w_tom_beliefs → Germany. The system can detect this divergence and flag it: Tom has a false belief about Paris.

scenarios.input: Step 4: 'What if gravity were 2x?' → fork w_2x_gravity (HYPOTHETICAL)
scenarios.reveal: w_2x_gravity inherits all of w0, overrides gravity.value = 19.6 m/s². This world is tagged HYPOTHETICAL — it's for reasoning, not belief. We can derive consequences (buildings need to be shorter, planes need more fuel) without polluting any belief world.

scenarios.input: Step 5: Cross-world query — 'What do we know about unusual freezing?'
scenarios.reveal:
The query router checks ALL worlds but TAGS the results by modality:
• w0 (ACTUAL): water freezes at 0°C, no unusual freezing
• w_cats_cradle (FICTIONAL): ice-nine freezes at room temp — SOURCE: Cat's Cradle
• w_tom_beliefs (EPISTEMIC): no freezing-related beliefs
• w_2x_gravity (HYPOTHETICAL): no freezing-related overrides

The system gives you a complete picture WITHOUT confusing fiction with reality.

props.title: Query Router — Which World Should Answer?
props.prompt: For each question, think about which world(s) the system should consult, and what modality type matters. Then reveal the answer.

scenarios.input: Question: 'At what temperature does water freeze?'
scenarios.reveal: Route to: w0 (ACTUAL) only. This is a factual question about the real world. Answer: 0°C. Fictional and epistemic worlds are irrelevant — we want ground truth.

scenarios.input: Question: 'What does Tom think about Paris?'
scenarios.reveal: Route to: w_tom_beliefs (EPISTEMIC). This is asking about a specific agent's beliefs, not about reality. Answer: Tom believes Paris is in Germany. The system might also flag: this diverges from w0 where Paris.country = France.

scenarios.input: Question: 'Is there any science fiction about unusual phase transitions?'
scenarios.reveal: Route to: ALL worlds with modality FICTIONAL. This is explicitly asking about fiction. Answer: In w_cats_cradle, ice-nine causes water to freeze at room temperature (source: Cat's Cradle by Kurt Vonnegut). The FICTIONAL tag is what makes this query possible without contaminating factual queries.

Route to: w_ethics (DEONTIC). This is a normative question — what SHOULD be true. Answer: No — there's an obligation against it. The system can also cross-reference with w_cats_cradle to explain WHY (the fictional consequences serve as cautionary evidence).

scenarios.input: Question: 'What would happen if we doubled gravity?'
scenarios.reveal: Route to: w_2x_gravity (HYPOTHETICAL). This is a counterfactual — reasoning about a world that doesn't exist. The system can derive consequences within this branch without affecting any other world. If the branch doesn't exist yet, the query might trigger its creation.

questions.question: Why can't an LLM that was trained on Cat's Cradle reliably distinguish ice-nine from real water physics?
options: LLMs store all knowledge in the same weight space with no ontological tags — facts and fiction are superimposed
Cat's Cradle is too similar to real physics textbooks
The model needs more training data to learn the difference
questions.explanation: LLMs represent all learned information as weight adjustments in the same parameter space. There's no structural distinction between 'this came from a physics textbook' and 'this came from a novel.' Both produce gradient updates that modify the same weights. The model has no metadata layer saying 'this is fictional' — it's all just statistical patterns.

questions.question: What's the lambda calculus equivalent of 'who has the ball?'
options: A database SELECT query with a WHERE clause
A fully applied lambda term with all arguments bound
A partially applied lambda term — ATRANS(?, ?, ball) — a function with unbound variables
A special query operator that searches the event log
questions.explanation: In the lambda store, queries are the SAME type as facts — they're lambda terms. A complete fact like ATRANS(Robert, Tom, ball) has all variables bound. A query like ATRANS(?, ?, ball) is partially applied — it has 'holes' (unbound variables) that match any value. This is the deep insight: data and queries are structurally identical, differing only in binding state.

questions.question: In the modal store, what modality type would you use for 'Tom thinks Paris is in Germany'?
options: FICTIONAL — it's not true, so it's fiction
EPISTEMIC — it represents what an agent believes, regardless of truth
REMEMBERED — it's something Tom vaguely recalls
questions.explanation: EPISTEMIC modality represents an agent's beliefs — what they hold to be true in their mental model of the world. Tom's belief that Paris is in Germany is false, but it's not fiction (Tom isn't writing a novel) and it's not hypothetical (Tom genuinely believes it). It's an epistemic state — a belief that happens to be wrong. The system can detect this by comparing Tom's epistemic world with the actual world.

questions.question: Why is the event log append-only instead of mutable?
options: Append-only preserves full history, enables time-travel queries, and makes retractions traceable — past states are always recoverable
questions.explanation: Immutability is a feature, not a limitation. An append-only log means: (1) you can always reconstruct any past state by replaying events up to that point, (2) retractions are themselves events with full provenance — you know WHEN something was retracted and WHY, (3) concurrent access is safe — no read-write conflicts, and (4) the complete history enables questions like 'who has EVER had the ball?' that mutable stores can't answer without extra bookkeeping.
